speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: He's eloquent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yes. Very good. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's eloquent. Are you OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were crying. I was trying to hold it in, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --because he has to look at me, and I-- I have to look like no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whenever you're ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, we're going to start with this. And I'm going to read this. I'm going to-- I'm going to lead into it, and then I want to read it. I want to read this page--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK--

speaker: interviewee
text: --up to here.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I have one quick suggestion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or two. Basically, you-- you know in your heart what you want to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And I know how I'm supposed to conduct the interview. So either-- either I lead you to the water, and it happens naturally, or as I'm leading you to the conversation, you can just bring in what you want to bring in--

speaker: interviewee
text: We're-- we're just--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and not-- not--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --worry, not worry, that you're highlighting everything--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but I'll-- I'll--

speaker: interviewer
text: --in your book.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I don't want to worry about that. But we're going into now the deportation which I witnessed. I'm easing with the deportation that I just witnessed, is--

speaker: interviewer
text: You've done a tremendous job so far.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm-- I'm ready whenever you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the end of the other tape was--

speaker: interviewer
text: The--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the field of [INAUDIBLE]--

speaker: interviewer
text: --you were talking about--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Regan ?] OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were talking about this fellow Becker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: But more importantly, where you--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. OK. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Horn, you were discussing the images of the Germans who were standing by at the deportation, and how youthful they looked. And as you were saying--

speaker: interviewee
text: Fresh faced. Fresh faced. Young. Handsome. The kind of boys you want to have live next door to you. That's the best description I can give you. But they-- they were determined, and they were brainwashed, and they thought they were on a holy mission. And they were convinced that, what it said on their buckles, [SPEAKING GERMAN] that 'God is with them,' and they're-- that what they're doing is right. That much I can tell you. Well, we passed all these columns. They had taken about 2,500 Jews out of the Walowa ghetto, and eliminated all 10,000 from the Glinice that night. OK, that night. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] These Ukrainian guards brought us into the ghetto, and they reported to one of the SS that was heading this thing, or he was some kind of an officer, who we are, where we come from, and why we're here. And the officer told him to put us into one of the yards and hold us there until he lets him know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were standing there for another 15-20 minutes. And then the order came that they had filled the quota for the day. That means that they no longer had any more room, any more freight cars, at the station. So the-- the whole action was suspended. And that was the end of the action. And we were given the order that we can go home. OK? So I'm standing there, and I can go home, and home is maybe three miles from that spot when we were released. And I say to myself, are my parents still at home? Do I still have a home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I didn't know what happened. I didn't know how many people they took out that day. I didn't know where they took them from.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I was released, I ran as fast as my legs would take me, hopped over fences, and came to the back of our house. And lo and behold, that part of town was completely peaceful. Completely peaceful, as if nothing happened. But the people were inside, in the houses, and nobody stuck his head out, because they were so scared. They knew that this action was taking place, and they were just waiting there next, I guess.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came across the very same rabbi who had performed my bar mitzvah rite, who lived in part of our complex, in the building. And he was standing outside, unafraid, with a tallit and tefillin on his head, and he was praying to God. And as I came upon him, he looked at me very strangely, and I looked at him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [BACKGROUND NOISE] somehow we embraced, because we knew-- somehow I feel that, in his face and in my face, the tragedy of the Jews that was happening to us was reflected. And he asked me what's going on, if I know what's happening. And I told him what I knew. And I knew then that my family was still safe, because he didn't know anything, and nothing had happened in that part of town yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I left him there, and I ran upstairs. We lived two flights up, on the second floor. And I opened the door, and I found my father, and my mother, and my sister Bluma.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all-- They were all dressed, ready to go, with a few bundles near the door. You know, couple of valises with backpack. And they were just sitting there waiting until the Germans come and they say, [SPEAKING GERMAN] which was-- that was the-- that was the call. And when I opened the door, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] they looked at me, and they couldn't believe it, that I made it back home. And I told them at the time what I had seen. Not what I had seen on the field, but what I had seen once I came back to the ghetto. I didn't want to disturb them with that. As young as I was, I knew I shouldn't do that. And I told them that it was all over, that they're not going to take for resettlement any more people that day, because they had filled the quota. And strangely, my father went out and he told the neighbors the good news. And all of a sudden, the signs of life returned to the building, as I remembered it as a child. And my mother told me to wash up. She prepared some food for me, and she told me to lie down and go to sleep, because she knew I had worked all night, and I needed the rest. And I want to [SIGHS] describe my experiences by reading this part of my memoirs now. [PAUSES FOR 22 SECONDS] My mother made me go to bed. She told me I had to rest after this terrible ordeal, and get ready for work again. I went to bed, but sleep eluded me. I hadn't told my parents of what I saw in the field that day, but I couldn't stop thinking about it. I remember thinking, in my adolescent innocence, that if I could just talk to Hitler, and explain how terribly people were suffering because of his policies, he would listen and bring a stop to it. I could not imagine that an enlightened, cultured, and educated people could make the cold-blooded murder of men, women, and children state policy. I was also kept awake by visions of my dear friends, whom I cherished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eventually, I got out of bed, and I saw my mother packing some of my things into a bag. 'Mother, what are you doing?' I asked. 'This is for you to take along to work in case they don't let you return to the ghetto.'

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I grabbed her hands and I said, 'No, wait, mama. I'm not going back to work. Whatever happens, we should stay together.' 'Don't be ridiculous. You have to go. Go and get washed.' And as on so many previous occasions, the vehemence of her answer precluded any further discussion. My mother finished my bag and prepared a plate of my favorite soup. She put some of the soup into a container that was part of an important tea set that was a treasured kitchen ornament. These containers had pictures of faraway exotic places on them, and they were always a part of my travel dreams. She put the container carefully in my bag so it wouldn't spill. When I finished eating, it was time to go. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I first approached Bluma to say goodbye. She had tears streaming down her face. Bluma was so attached to my mother that she would not hear of going to work to obtain an exemption from deportation. She made it crystal clear from the time the rumors started that she would stay with my mother and father no matter what. She was 19 years old. I was actually glad Bluma was with my parents. It helps ease the guilt pangs I still suffer to this day for having, in effect, abandoned them myself. Bluma handed me two gold coins that had been camouflaged as covered buttons on a sweater she wore. I knew that these coins were the family's only remaining valuables, and I refused to take them. I kissed Bluma's tear stained face and turned towards my mother. For the first time in my life, I saw my mother's face without her stern composure. She gathered me in her arms, and for the first time ever told me how much she loved me, and how proud she was of me. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] She told me to live by the values she taught me. She put her hands over my head, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] blessed me, and wished me God's speed. The hardest of all was to say goodbye to my father. To this day, I think that watching my father disintegrate and suffer affected me more than any other experience. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I just couldn't bear to see him helpless. I embraced him silently. We looked at each other, knowing full well that it was the last time. Then, he patted me gently on the head, as he had so many times before. He tried to speak, but all that came out was a violent sob. I grabbed my bag, turned on my heels, and left my home. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] [CRYING] Sorry. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you go to work at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went back to the munitions factory, and that night, my mother was right. They wouldn't let us go back to the ghetto, and they confined us to the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Quarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the quarters of the-- of the munitions factory. Terrible, terrible, terrible conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- who were you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I was not well. I was not good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have anyone you knew that could comfort you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I-- I couldn't see anyone. I-- I-- I was a wreck. I was a total wreck. All I could think of is why I abandoned them, why I didn't go with them, and I was very sorry that I did. I didn't think I had the right to stay by myself, and it was one of the lowest points in my odyssey of survival. I just didn't want to go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were how old then? It was 1942 or 3?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear what was going on outside where you were? Could you-- was there any-- were you close by to where there trains were, where the ammunitions factory was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't for another 10 days or so, till August 16, that they got ready for the rest of the people in Radom. On August 16, they installed reflectors, so that they could work day and night in the ghetto. And August 16, 17, and 18, in three days, working day and night, [SIGHS] they emptied the ghetto in Radom. There must've been at least 23,000, 24,000 people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that the group that your parents and sister were taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, that was the group that my parents and sister were taken. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were restricted to the ammunitions factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was restricted to ammunition factory, yes. I was-- night--

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were restricted to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I worked nights, as I told you, and the factory machines would stop for lunch in the middle of the night. And it was in the summer time. It was August, and the windows were open, so we clearly hear gunfire that was coming from the ghetto. And we looked at, and we could see, the lights, the reflectors, that they had put in, you know, to be able to work at night. We could see that from afar. It was like a glow of fire. And I could barely function at that time. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Out of the ghettos in Walowa and Glinice, which comprised over 35,000 Jews, after the resettlement action that took place on August 16, there were approximately 3,000 Jews left that were put into a two block area, which was called Targowiska, in one corner of the former ghetto. And that was the end of the, you might say, of the Jewish community in Radom really.

speaker: interviewer
text: And those 3,000 people, were they eventually shipped away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those 3,000 were eventually shipped away as well. And there was a third deportation, which took place later on. But basically, they left the 3,000 people for a simple reason, that they needed workers--

speaker: interviewer
text: To clean up.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to clean up and process. And so after this deportation, they took the people that they left over in this two block area, and under the direction of the Germans, they went house by house, block by block, street by street, and they stripped everything that was left over, and took everything that was worthwhile, packed it, and shipped it to Germany. And that was the main reason why they left the 3,000 people, to do cleanup work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, where were the 3,000 cleanup workers sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were also kept for another reason. Some of the people that were left among the 3,000 were tradesmen. They were-- they were tailors, they were shoemakers, they were furriers, they were knitters, they were people who knew how to put things together. And these, the SS hierarchy that was in charge of the Final Solution, left those people, if for no other reason is so that they would continue doing stuff for them, making things for them, which they eventually shipped home and enriched themselves with. Because looking back at it from-- with hindsight, I can see now that the toyed with Jews like a cat toys with a mouse. They figured these Jews are condemned anyway. They're going to die sooner or later. Why can't they make some leather coats for us, or why can't they make some uniforms for us, or why can't they-- why can't they make some furniture for us, and so on and so forth. And this is what they used them for.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the 3,000 people that were left in the ghetto were being eliminated slowly. And they had different, what we called actions. You know, like they would come in-- I know that two of my uncles that survived, two of my uncles-- one was my father's brother, Hershel. And one was my mother's brother-in-law, a-- a-- a gentleman, [? Eric ?] [? Rothsfeld. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- they survived the initial resettlements. And they were both in that ghetto. But they were later selected. You know, the people who were older, people who were weak, people who were-- you know, they kept selecting, taking them out, and shooting them. Taking them out, and shooting them. In other words, people who couldn't provide them with the services that they needed were completely unnecessary and removed. That was the existence of the people of the 3,000 in the ghetto. As far as my fate was concerned, I stayed in the munitions factory, and I completely deteriorated. And primarily I deteriorated because life was not worth living. And I felt terrible guilt pangs, and I stopped caring about my appearance. And I showed all the signs of going, which was commonplace. Other people showed it, too. I didn't want to see or talk to anybody that knew me before, or that I knew before. I was ashamed of the way I looked. I was ashamed-- I-- I-- I just didn't want to go on. I had no reason to go on. I lost completely the will to survive. But then, one day, one of my friends, who remained among the 3,000, sent me a note through a Jewish auxiliary, who was a liaison between the ghetto-- the remaining ghetto, and the munitions factory. And this fellow looked me up, and he handed me the note. And in the note, my friend, Moniek Ackerman told me that he heard that I was down and out, and that he wants to help me. And he told me to make an attempt, an escape from the munitions factory into the ghetto. And if I managed to do that, he will be able to hide me, and he will take care of me. Well, I loved this boy. This was my best friend. We grew up together, and we had much fun together. And this note gave me [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] new hope, new courage. And as soon as I got this note, I-- I turned around. I began caring for my appearance, and I-- I-- I tried to eat a little better, organize some food for myself. Eventually, he managed to send me some money also, through the same auxiliary. So I was able to buy myself some food. I was able to share some of that money with my brother, Ely, who was also in the munitions factory at the time. But he worked day shift, I worked night shift, so we could hardly ever see each other. [SIGHS] And I started thinking about the escape. And eventually, I managed to escape, which in itself was quite an experience. But I did escape one evening, and I made it to the ghetto. And-- and I made it to Moniek Ackerman's home in the ghetto. And he took me upstairs. They lived in a-- in a house, and they had a attic. He took me upstairs to the attic. And he had set up-- in a corner of the attic, he had set up a-- an old sofa, which served as a bed, with a little table, with a lamp on it. And he took me to that corner of the attic, and he says, this is where you're going to stay. And he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was a good friend of my wife's as well. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I stayed there for quite some time. And I was safe there, because I wasn't registered in the ghetto, and I had no right to be there. And if I didn't have a place like this, I would be caught and sent back to the munitions factory for punishment for the escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were just two children taking care of each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was no one else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he-- no, no, no, no. He had his family. His family was survived intact. And they were still well to do. They still had money. For some reason, his father had made friends with an influential SS man. And that SS man had warned him continually about ongoing actions. So he-- he was able to shelter himself and his family so far. And I became the beneficiary of that, because they would not go to-- into that house to search, because these people were what we considered to be influential. And so I stayed with him until January of '43. January of '43, we woke up in the morning, in the dead of winter, [SIGHS] to the screams of [SPEAKING GERMAN]. By that time, that was a familiar saying. [SPEAKING GERMAN] means that it was time for another resettlement or deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: The translation of [SPEAKING GERMAN]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's not a translation. [SPEAKING GERMAN] actually means, 'All Jews out.' But it was the-- that was it. They want all Jews out, because they want to select to see who goes where. And the third deportation began on January 13. January 13, they took out about 1,500 people from that small ghetto. At that point, perhaps 1,000 were left, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after the third deportation on January 13, which I also witnessed, which I describe in detail in my book. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After that, I could no longer stay with Moniek and his family, because he told me that they were going to escape to Warsaw. They were able to secure Aryan papers. And they did. And I got myself, with-- you know, with his influence, I got myself a little room in a basement. But I didn't stay there long, because in the beginning-- on the 1st of March, two auxiliary policemen came down to that basement room where I stayed, and they told me to pack whatever I had. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they needed 80 people to go to this place called Blizyn to start a new concentration camp. I was among the 80, and this is the new setting that I was sent. That was Blizyn, about 20 kilometers from Radom. A new concentration camp was being built there, and I was part of the 80 people who went there to actually open it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Blizyn, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-L-I-Z-Y-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
